samples.top_objection: I don't trust that the photo detection will reliably tell apart similar SKUs on a packed shelf without a ton of training

samples.private_reasoning_excerpt: Look, I run a small specialty shop and I do shelf walks twice a day with a clipboard. If snapping a few phone pics could actually spit out a reliable restock list and flag the SKUs that keep going to zero, $79 a month is nothing compared to what I lose when my top-moving items are out on a Saturday. samples.top_objection: Taking daily shelf photos might be more hassle than just eyeballing what's low while I restock.
samples.top_proof_need: A short video or case from a real independent store showing the accuracy on messy, mixed shelves over a few weeks.
samples.private_reasoning_excerpt: Look, I got burned by Sortly after their last update jacked things up, so I'm wary of any new inventory tool promising the moon. The phone-photo idea is clever and $79 isn't crazy if it actually works, but I have no idea how accurate the shelf detection really is on a cluttered aisle with mixed SKUs, glare, and price tags hanging down. samples.top_objection: I don't trust shelf detection accuracy on cluttered, multi-facing shelves until I see it work on a store like mine

samples.private_reasoning_excerpt: Honestly, the photo-based approach could save me a real chunk of time if the detection actually works on my crowded shelves with handwritten price tags and mixed SKUs. $79 a month isn't crazy if it catches even a couple of stockouts a week that I'd otherwise miss. But I've been burned by 'AI' tools before that demo great and fall apart on messy real-world shelves, so I'd want to see it run on a store that looks like mine before I commit. samples.top_objection: Daily photo routine will fall apart with my staff, and accuracy on tightly packed shelves is unproven.
samples.top_proof_need: A free trial or short pilot in a similar small store showing real detection accuracy and how long the photo walk actually takes.
samples.private_reasoning_excerpt: Honestly, the idea is interesting and $79 isn't crazy money if it actually saves me from running out of fast-movers. But I keep coming back to the photo-taking part — who's actually going to walk every aisle snapping pics consistently? My staff barely keeps the cooler faced. And without seeing how accurate the detection really is on a crowded shelf with similar SKUs, I'm not handing over my restock list to it. I'd want to see real proof that the photo detection is accurate on messy real-world shelves — not a demo store — and proof that it actually catches stockouts I'd miss.
